summarization.a: Figure 5 shows an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey scan of the SiO₂ surface after complete removal of the TiN film by the CCl₄-based CVE process at 460 °C. The surface is primarily composed of silicon and oxygen, with small amounts of carbon (~5.1 at%) and nitrogen (~1.2 at%). Titanium and chlorine are below the detection limit (<0.1 at%). The nitrogen and carbon signals likely arise from surface contamination.

data_extraction.a:
| Binding energy [eV] | Element | Relative intensity [a.u.] |
|---|---|---|
| 284.8 | C 1s | Detected |
| 399.1 | N 1s | Detected |
| 532.0 | O 1s | Detected |
| 103.3 | Si 2p | Detected |

a.question: Does the CCl₄-based CVE process at 460 °C completely remove the TiN film from the SiO₂ surface?
How does the XPS elemental composition of the SiO₂ surface compare after the CCl₄-based CVE etch in terms of titanium and chlorine presence?
a.answer: After the etch, the XPS analysis shows that titanium and chlorine are below the detection limit (<0.1 at%), indicating complete removal of TiN and no residual chlorine contamination. In contrast, silicon and oxygen dominate the surface composition, with minor amounts of carbon and nitrogen likely due to adventitious surface contamination. This shows that the etch effectively removes the TiN while leaving the SiO₂ substrate intact.

a.question: What could be the source of the small carbon and nitrogen signals detected on the post-etch SiO₂ surface?
a.answer: Surface contamination from handling, Sample storage and transportation in non-cleanroom conditions, Adventitious organic materials adsorbed on the surface
